interpretations.interpretation: As a straightforward genealogical statement: Jacob is the father of Joseph, Joseph is the husband of Mary, and Mary is the mother of Jesus—so the audience would read it as establishing family lineage and identity for Jesus.

As a claim of legitimate status: by naming preceding relations (Jacob → Joseph → Mary → Jesus), the text signals that Jesus belongs to a known family line, which would matter to listeners concerned with rightful origins.

As identifying Jesus by title: the phrase “the one called Christ” would lead many in an early Christian audience to understand that Jesus is recognized as the promised/appointed Messiah (“Christ”), not merely a random teacher.
As linking Jesus’s birth to Mary’s role: since Mary is explicitly named as the one from whom Jesus is born, listeners would take the verse as emphasizing Mary as the biological mother of Jesus.

As part of a larger fulfillment narrative: this kind of genealogical opening would likely be heard within the broader expectation that God’s promises unfold through specific people in history, so the audience may anticipate that Jesus’s life connects to earlier scriptural hopes.
